issues.title: Two adjacent edge-label plates overlap ('batch load' / 'feed'), clipping text
issues.description: Hands-on rendering of the acceptance-signal repro (scratchpad/repro.mmd) to static PNG/SVG (light, dark, fancy themes) AND the interactive HTML export shows two DIFFERENT edges' labels overlapping each other: 'IN -->|batch load| K1' and 'API -->|feed| V1' route as near-parallel vertical channels close together, and their label plates collide. Exact geometry read from the rendered label-layer: the 'batch load' plate spans x=348.25..438.25 (SVG units) and the 'feed' plate spans x=413.45..453.05 - a 24.8px overlap region. Visually the 'd' in 'load' is clipped/hidden behind the opaque 'feed' plate, and the two texts read as a run-together 'batch loa feed' with no gap. Reproduced identically across --theme light/dark/fancy and --style clean/sketch, and in both the static PNG/SVG (svg.ts) and the interactive HTML export (dom/runtime.ts) - i.e. it is a shared layout-level defect (label positions come from the same geometry/layout computation both renderers consume), not a per-renderer regression. This is distinct from FR1-FR4 in plan.md, which fixed edge-over-label and edge-over-title occlusion and tightened per-label padding, but did not add any check for two DIFFERENT edge labels landing close enough together to overlap each other.
issues.proposed_solution: FIXABLE. When two (or more) edges route as near-parallel close-spaced channels (as here, both funnelling into the two subgraphs from the top), their label midpoints can land within a plate-width of each other. Suggest detecting/avoiding this at label placement time: either (a) widen the horizontal separation between adjacent near-parallel edge midpoints before assigning label x-position (analogous to the FR4 perimeter-spread fix, but for label lanes rather than port anchors), or (b) after computing all edge-label plate rects for a diagram, detect AABB overlap between any two plates and nudge one vertically to a different label row/lane (similar in spirit to the existing per-edge stagger math in layout/index.ts's labelPlateSize/stagger). Verify with a new unit assertion (e.g. in test/layout.test.ts or test/render-svg.test.ts) that asserts no two edge-label plate rects intersect for a fixture with close parallel edges, and re-render scratchpad/repro.mmd to confirm 'batch load' and 'feed' are both fully legible with a visible gap.
issues.fix_summary: Fixed via the re-planned FR6 (resolveLabelCollisions): a shared, deterministic all-pairs edge-label plate de-collision pass in src/geometry/index.ts, wired via layout.finishEdges() into layout()/applyPositions() and the native state re-route, and mirrored byte-for-byte in the runtime twin (buildSvg/renderEdges). It de-collides from the ROUNDED plate centre so shifts stay byte-parity. Re-rendering scratchpad/repro.mmd now shows 'batch load' and 'feed' on separate rows with a visible gap. New assertions: geometry resolveLabelCollisions unit tests + a layout invariant 'no two edge-label plates overlap' across all fixtures + the TEST-001 repro shape. dom-runtime-parity stays green.
issues.verification_note: Re-tested round 2 hands-on via the gogo-playwright MCP against a fresh HTML export of scratchpad/repro.mmd (served over a local static HTTP server, file:// still blocked by the MCP navigate tool): browser_evaluate read the two plates' real getBoundingClientRect() geometry — 'batch load' plate y=287.44..313.72, 'feed' plate y=321.60..347.88 — an AABB check confirms ZERO overlap on either axis, with a ~7.9px visible vertical gap between the two rows; a full-page screenshot confirms both labels are fully legible with a clean visual gap (no clipping, no run-together text). Re-confirmed identically across all 6 static PNG combos (light/dark/fancy x clean/sketch) and 0 console errors in the interactive HTML export. Fix holds — TEST-001 is closed.

issues.title: Pre-existing e2e/sketch.spec.ts had 3 stale direct-child selectors broken by the FR1 layer-group DOM restructure (fixed this round)
issues.description: npm run test:e2e initially failed 3 of 73 tests, all in the pre-existing e2e/sketch.spec.ts (from the earlier 'sketch-style' feature, unmodified by this feature's diff): 'renders hand-drawn wavy node outlines and edges (not crisp rects)', 'dragging a node re-routes its rough edges live and keeps the wobble', and 'class: renders hand-drawn card outlines without console errors'. Root cause: FR1's 5-layer draw-order restructure (this feature) moved every edge/node <path> from a direct child of <svg class="vnm-edges"> into a nested <g class="vnm-edge-layer">/<g class="vnm-node-layer"> (see src/render/dom/runtime.ts:197-212). The 3 failing assertions used a direct-child CSS combinator (`svg.vnm-edges > path`), which now matches 0 elements even though the paths render correctly one level deeper. Verified via the gogo-playwright MCP against the built sketch-flowchart export: `svg.vnm-edges` has 0 direct-child <path>s but 54 descendant <path>s (42 stroked), and a full-page screenshot confirms the hand-drawn sketch flowchart renders visually correct (wobbly outlines, open arrowheads, all nodes/edges legible) - this is a pure test-selector staleness issue, not a functional/visual regression. review-01.md's dimension-6 pass ('Layer-group DOM restructure - CLEAN... the only consumer of the former flat structure - the interactive-ports test helper - was updated') missed that e2e/sketch.spec.ts was also a consumer of the flat structure.
issues.proposed_solution: FIXED IN THIS ROUND (test-file-only change, no product code touched): updated the 3 selectors in e2e/sketch.spec.ts from the direct-child combinator (`svg.vnm-edges > path...`) to a descendant selector (`svg.vnm-edges path...`), matching the new (reviewed, intentional) FR1 layer-group nesting. Re-ran `npm run test:e2e`: all 73 tests pass, 0 failures.
issues.fix_summary: Updated the 3 selectors in e2e/sketch.spec.ts from the direct-child combinator (svg.vnm-edges > path...) to a descendant selector (svg.vnm-edges path...), matching the new intentional FR1 layer-group nesting (g.vnm-edge-layer / g.vnm-node-layer). Test-file-only change, no product code touched. Re-ran npm run test:e2e: 73/73 pass.

issues.title: e2e/interactions.spec.ts had a stale 'elbow paths never contain C or Q' assertion invalidated by the new FR7 bridge-hop behaviour (fixed this round)
issues.description: npm run test:e2e initially failed 1 of 73 tests after the round-2 FR6/FR7 changes: 'the fancy theme routes curved edges; light routes orthogonal elbows' (e2e/interactions.spec.ts:145-162, pre-existing from the original FR1-FR5 feature). The assertion `expect(elbowDs.every((d) => !/[CQ]/.test(d))).toBe(true)` encoded 'orthogonal elbow paths never use C (cubic bezier) or Q (quadratic bezier) path commands' — true before FR7, but FR7's D2 arc-hop bridges deliberately splice a small `Q` bump into the hopping edge's `d` at a genuine crossing, and D4 defaults bridges ON for exactly this diagram's theme (light = clean elbow). Root-caused (not guessed) by rendering fixtures/state-machine.mmd (the fixture this test drives) via renderSvgAsync and inspecting each edge's `d`: 2 of 7 edges carry a `Q` bump (paths index 3 and 5), matching the plan's own note that state-machine.mmd 'is expected to have 2 crossings' (per the round-2 test brief). This is a genuine, INTENDED consequence of the newly-shipped FR7 behaviour, not a functional regression — the diagram's edges are still fundamentally orthogonal line segments (every `d` still contains `L`), just with a small legitimate arc bump at the two unavoidable crossings.
issues.proposed_solution: FIXED IN THIS ROUND (test-file-only change, no product code touched): narrowed the assertion in e2e/interactions.spec.ts to `expect(elbowDs.every((d) => !/C/.test(d))).toBe(true)` (elbow paths never use a full cubic bezier — that's still reserved for the fancy/curved theme) and added a positive assertion `expect(elbowDs.some((d) => /Q/.test(d))).toBe(true)` (state-machine.mmd's genuine crossings should produce at least one FR7 bridge hop), keeping the original `some(d => /L/.test(d))` line-segment check. Re-ran npm run test:e2e: all 73 (now 79, see the new FR6/FR7 spec) pass. Also added a dedicated e2e/bridges-and-labels.spec.ts (6 new tests) covering FR6 (repro's batch-load/feed gap, across themes), FR7 (exactly-one-hop on a genuine crossing, live drag re-route keeps exactly one hop, --no-bridges strips it), and D5 (sequence diagrams get zero bridge glyphs) per the plan's Tests table e2e row.
issues.fix_summary: Narrowed e2e/interactions.spec.ts's elbow-path assertion from 'no C or Q' to 'no C, but Q from an FR7 bridge hop is expected and asserted present' — a test-file-only change reflecting the new, intended FR7 behaviour (bridges default ON for clean elbow, D4). No product code touched. Re-ran npm run test:e2e: 73/73 (then 79/79 after also adding e2e/bridges-and-labels.spec.ts), 0 failures.

issues.title: FR9 lane separation can fail to re-engage after a live node drag, reverting to the pre-fix merged-line defect
issues.description:
The plan's Tests table requires 'lines stay separated after dragging a node (live re-route)' and the BDD scenario requires 'no two of those runs sit within LANE_GAP of each other' to hold after interactive edits, not just on the initial render. Reproduced two independent ways: (1) live browser via the gogo-playwright MCP — opened the interactive HTML export of scratchpad/repro.mmd, dragged the Ingress node with a real dragTo (real pointer events) to open canvas space (~world (138,320)); the IN->K1 'batch load' / IN->HUB / API->V1 'feed' mid-channel gaps collapsed from the correct 26px lane spacing back to 20px — the exact pre-fix defect width from uat.md round 1. (2) direct-library debug harness (scratchpad/test-out-round3/debug-lanes.mts, tsx calling layout()/applyPositions()/separateLanes straight from src/, not the compiled bundle, not guessed): original undragged gaps are 26/26 (matches LANE_GAP=26, src/geometry/index.ts:631); after applyPositions({IN:{x:138,y:320}}) (an ordinary reposition, not an edge case) the gaps become 20/20. Calling separateLanes a second time on the already-processed result only partially shifts things and still does not converge to clean 26px lanes, confirming genuine under-convergence (LANE_PASSES=1, src/geometry/index.ts:637, is insufficient for some post-reposition geometries), not a legitimate 'bundle no longer qualifies' case. A smaller drag (IN -> {138,90}, ~70px nudge) does NOT trigger it, so this is geometry-dependent but reachable via ordinary editing, not a contrived adversarial input. A plausible contributing factor (not fully proven): hand-tracing the bundle-detection loop (src/geometry/index.ts:687-726) against the post-drag segment list shows the IN->HUB edge can contribute two different segments (its two separate vertical runs) to the same bundle transitively, which may dilute/destabilize the mean/slot computation for the three genuinely-different edges that need separating. Box 3 (hub fan-in, anchor-spread via computePerimeterPorts, not the lane-bundle mechanism) is unaffected by the same drag. Both static and live-runtime reproductions show the same qualitative collapse, so this is a shared separateLanes algorithm gap, not a runtime-twin byte-parity drift.

ROUND 4 RE-TEST — REOPENED, fix does not hold for the reported repro. The round-3 fix (per-edge dedup + LANE_PASSES 1→4) genuinely improves robustness for small/moderate repositions — confirmed via the new layout.test.ts regression test's 4 deltas ([40,0],[0,30],[-30,20],[60,-10] from IN's base position), all hold at 26px — but those 4 deltas are all mild nudges that keep IN within ~30-60px of its original (188.5,29) position. They do NOT reproduce the actual failing geometry from round 3. Re-ran the EXACT round-3 repro (IN -> world (138,320), the coordinates test-03.md itself used) two ways: (a) round-3's own scratchpad/test-out-round3/debug-lanes.mts, unmodified, re-executed against the current fixed src/ — still reports 'batch load' x=397.25 (y=[174,320]), 'IN→HUB' x=417.25 (y=[80,226]), 'feed' x=437.25 (y=[80,200]) → gaps 20, 20, byte-identical to the pre-fix round-3 numbers; (b) live in the interactive HTML runtime (gogo-playwright MCP, a real page.mouse down/move(8 steps)/up drag, not a synthetic event, landing the node dead-on at world (138,320) — confirmed via the DOM's left/top style) — same qualitative collapse, 'batch load' x=393.25, 'IN→HUB' x=413.25, 'feed' x=433.25 → gaps 20, 20 (a constant ~4px offset vs. the Node script from real browser font metrics, but identical gaps), 0 app-level console errors (only the known benign favicon 404), confirmed visually in a full-page screenshot and a cropped close-up — the three lines are visually merged/hard to trace, the same qualitative defect the whole feature exists to fix. ROOT-CAUSED (not guessed) via a bundle-detection trace script that replays separateLanes' exact sort/bundle/dedup logic against the final post-fix edge list: the mid-channel bundle never forms at all in this geometry. Sorted by x, the interior vertical segments are IN→HUB's OTHER (structurally unrelated) segment at x=376 (y=[226,422]), then 'batch load' at x=397.25 (y=[174,320], 21.25px away, near → bundled), then IN→HUB's true mid-channel segment at x=417.25 (y=[80,226], 20px from 'batch load' → near via 'batch load' → CONSUMED but, per the round-3 fix's own 'at most one segment per edge in a bundle' dedup rule, NOT added to the bundle array because edge IN→HUB already has a member at x=376), then 'feed' at x=437.25 (y=[80,200]) — 40px from 'batch load' (not < LANE_GAP) and no longer able to bridge via the x=417.25 segment because that segment was excluded from the bundle array by the dedup. Net: the 3-run bundle {batch load, IN→HUB-mid, feed} that SHOULD form and spread to 26px lanes instead splits into a 2-member bundle ({x=376, batch-load}, below LANE_MIN_BUNDLE=3) that gets skipped entirely, leaving 'batch load'/IN→HUB-mid/feed exactly where geometry put them (20/20). This is a genuine, newly-diagnosed side effect of the TEST-004 fix's own dedup mechanism, not a pre-existing gap the fix simply didn't reach — it is an order-dependent (sort-by-x) interaction between dedup and a multi-segment edge whose two segments are relevant to two DIFFERENT bundles. Scanned other repositions to characterize scope: (307,212)→20/20 gap (also fails), (400,400)→6px gap (fails worse), (50,500)→26.5px (holds), (138,90)→26px (holds, matches round 3's own note) — confirms this is geometry-dependent, reachable via ordinary editing, and specifically includes the primary reported repro. No other regression found: 384/384 unit + 79/79 e2e still green; determinism holds (byte-identical re-renders, light/fancy/sketch); the static (undragged) repro still shows the correct 26/26 mid-channel + clean box 3 hub-fan + disclosed-residual box 1, matching round 3 exactly, no new x≈499 drift; fixtures (state-machine/order-state/shop-class/microservices) render clean, no churn; box 1 and box 3 are unaffected by this drag (box 3's 6 hub arrows stay visually distinct in the screenshot).
issues.proposed_solution: FIXABLE within the already-accepted D6-D9 approach (not a new design fork/scope question) — but the round-3 fix's dedup rule needs to be less naive about a multi-segment edge that legitimately belongs to two different bundles. Suggested directions for the implementer to evaluate, given the round-4 root cause: (a) when a segment of an edge already 'used' in one bundle is found near a DIFFERENT, still-forming bundle, don't just silently drop it — either allow it to seed/join the second bundle too (a segment, not an edge, is the real unit of bundling; the zig-zag concern from the original TEST-004 diagnosis was about a SINGLE bundle containing two segments of the same edge, not about one edge legitimately touching two disjoint bundles), or re-run bundle detection after a bundle is resolved so the freed-up segment gets a fair second look in the same pass; (b) alternatively, tie-break bundle formation by which grouping actually needs the segment (e.g. prefer joining the segment to whichever candidate bundle would otherwise fall below LANE_MIN_BUNDLE) rather than pure first-come sort-by-x order; (c) MOST IMPORTANT — whatever the fix, add a regression test that uses the ACTUAL round-3 repro coordinates (IN -> {x:138,y:320}, not a mild delta) and asserts the mid-channel gap stays >= LANE_GAP; the round-3 fix's own shipped test in layout.test.ts (the 4 deltas [40,0]/[0,30]/[-30,20]/[60,-10]) all keep IN within ~60px of its start position and never actually exercise this failure mode — that test should be extended (not replaced) with the (138,320) case and 1-2 of the other newly-found failing repositions ((307,212), (400,400)) so this exact regression can't resurface silently again. Evidence: scratchpad/test-out-round3/debug-lanes.mts (round-3's original harness, re-run unmodified and still reproduces the defect), scratchpad/test-out-round4/trace-bundles.mts (new — root-cause bundle-formation trace), scratchpad/test-out-round4/verify-lanes.mts + verify-shipped-test-deltas.mts (new — shows the shipped test's deltas don't exercise the failure), scratchpad/test-out-round4/repro-dragged-138-320-full.png + crop-midchannel-dragged.png (live-browser visual confirmation).
issues.fix_summary: User accepted shipping with this as a DOCUMENTED FOLLOW-UP (D13, 2026-07-13). The extreme-drag lane re-merge only manifests when a node is hauled far out of its layout; all user-reported defects (mid-channel, hub, gaps, gRPC label, API-fan knot) are fixed. The fix is the deferred comb-stagger for endpoint fans (declined at D9/D10/D11). Tracked as a follow-up, not resolved.
issues.retest_note: NOT verified — reopened. The fix holds for the shipped unit test's own mild deltas but fails on the primary reported repro (IN -> world (138,320)), reproduced independently via round-3's own unmodified debug harness and a live browser drag (real page.mouse events, exact target landing confirmed, 0 console errors). See description for the full round-4 evidence and root-cause trace.
issues.round6_note: NOT re-tested this round — round 6 is explicitly scoped to the D12 API-fan de-knot only (per the orchestrator's task framing, this drag/lane-convergence limitation is carried forward as a pre-existing disclosed limitation from the D11 UAT decision, not part of D12). Status left exactly as round 4 left it (open, unverified) pending an explicit scope/priority call — not silently closed or dispositioned by this round.

issues.title: e2e/interactions.spec.ts still asserted the pre-pivot 'Q bridge-hop' glyph after FR7 moved to pen-up crossing GAPS (stale, found+fixed this round)
issues.description: npm run test:e2e initially failed 'the fancy theme routes curved edges; light routes orthogonal elbows' (e2e/interactions.spec.ts:145-167). The assertion `expect(elbowDs.some((d) => /Q/.test(d))).toBe(true)` (added at TEST-003, round 2, when FR7's crossing glyph was still the D2 arc-bump `Q`) was never updated after the UAT-round D11 redirect ("instead of bridges/elbows lets make it look like this so just a space") pivoted FR7's glyph from an arc bump to a pen-up GAP (an `L` endpoint immediately followed by an `M` restart — see e2e/bridges-and-labels.spec.ts's own `GAP_RE`). That pivot shipped at implement round 7 (reviewed/approved at review round 5), but no e2e round ran between round 7 and the D12 UAT finding, so this staleness was never caught until this round. Rendering fixtures/state-machine.mmd (the fixture this test drives) confirms it still has genuine unavoidable crossings and correctly emits gap breaks — the diagram itself is fine; only the test's glyph assumption (Q, not gap) was wrong. Root-caused (not guessed): confirmed via a live render that elbowDs contains zero `Q` characters but does contain the `L .. M ..` gap pattern. Unrelated to D12 (this round's actual scope) — the drift predates D12 by one full implement round.
issues.proposed_solution: FIXED IN THIS ROUND (test-file-only change, no product code touched): updated e2e/interactions.spec.ts:145-167 to (a) fold the Q-check into the existing C-check (`!/[CQ]/.test(d)` — orthogonal elbows never use a curve command of either kind) since Q is no longer a legitimate elbow output at all, and (b) replace the stale positive `/Q/` assertion with a positive check for the current gap pattern (`/ L [-\d.]+ [-\d.]+ M [-\d.]+ [-\d.]+/`, matching bridges-and-labels.spec.ts's GAP_RE) so the test still proves state-machine.mmd's genuine crossings produce a visible crossing glyph — just the correct one. Also updated the stale in-line comment referencing 'D2 arc bump'. Re-ran npm run test:e2e: 79/79 pass.
issues.fix_summary: Updated e2e/interactions.spec.ts's elbow-path assertions: merged the Q-check into the existing no-cubic-bezier check (`!/[CQ]/`) and replaced the stale positive `/Q/.test(d)` assertion with a positive check for the current pen-up GAP_RE pattern, reflecting FR7's round-7 arc→gap pivot (D11). Test-file-only, no product code touched. Re-ran npm run test:e2e: 79/79 pass, 0 failures.

issues.title: e2e/bridges-and-labels.spec.ts assumed CROSSING_DSL forces exactly one crossing; the shipped layout now produces two genuine crossings (stale count, found+fixed this round)
issues.description: npm run test:e2e initially failed 'a diagram with one genuine crossing renders exactly one gapped edge, 0 console errors' (e2e/bridges-and-labels.spec.ts:122-138): `expect(gappedCount).toBe(1)` received 2. CROSSING_DSL (`X-->M;Y-->M;M-->P;M-->Q;X-->Q;Y-->P`) was designed (and documented in test-strategy.md as '(1 hop)') around a single forced X-->Q vs Y-->P crossing back when the glyph was an arc bump; the routing that has evolved across several LATER implement rounds (FR9 lane offsets, hub PORT_STEP/border-fill spread) now genuinely produces TWO independent perpendicular crossings for this fixture in the current layout: X-->Q's early vertical run crosses Y-->M's horizontal jog (~137,67), and separately M-->P's vertical run crosses X-->Q's later horizontal jog (~114.25,182). Both hand-verified by tracing every edge's `d` coordinates — neither is a touching-at-endpoint or parallel-graze false positive, both are genuine interior perpendicular intersections, and FR7 correctly gaps each one (the qualitative behaviour is right; only the fixture's 'exactly one' assumption is now stale). ROOT-CAUSED AS UNRELATED TO D12 (this round's actual scope): re-rendered the same fixture with the D12 heading-order `bends` parameter temporarily disabled at both `computePerimeterPorts` call sites in src/layout/index.ts, rebuilt, and re-rendered — produced the byte-identical 2-gap output, confirming this is pre-existing drift from the FR9/hub-spread rounds, not a D12 regression. (Immediately reverted the temporary edit; typecheck + build re-confirmed clean afterward.) The adjacent drag-reroute test in the same file (line 141) was NOT affected — it happens to collapse back to 1 crossing after M is dragged, so it already passed.
issues.proposed_solution: FIXED IN THIS ROUND (test-file-only change, no product code touched): updated the assertion from `expect(gappedCount).toBe(1)` to `expect(gappedCount).toBe(2)`, renamed the test from 'renders exactly one gapped edge' to 'renders a gapped edge at each one' (genuine crossings, plural), and added an inline comment recording both crossing coordinates plus the D12-disabled control-test result so a future reader doesn't have to re-derive it. Left the DSL and the drag/toggle tests untouched. Re-ran npm run test:e2e: 79/79 pass. Flagged for the record (not a blocker): test-strategy.md's line 78 still describes this fixture as '(1 hop)' — now stale; worth a one-line correction at the next knowledge sync (phase ⑤), out of the tester's scope to edit directly.
issues.fix_summary: Updated e2e/bridges-and-labels.spec.ts's crossing-count assertion from `toBe(1)` to `toBe(2)` (verified genuinely correct for the current shipped geometry — two real, independent, perpendicular line crossings, each correctly gapped by FR7) and updated the test title/comments accordingly. Confirmed unrelated to D12 via a disable-bends control test (byte-identical 2-gap output with D12's heading-order ports on or off). Test-file-only, no product code touched. Re-ran npm run test:e2e: 79/79 pass, 0 failures.
